docs.title: Wong Kin Chong O/A level Notes
docs.text: Welcome to my notes website. I am Kin Chong, currently studying Computer Science in NUS. On the side, I give tuition for O level and A level students on math and sciences.

docs.title: What's this website for
docs.text:
This website is created to support my students in their revision and independent learning. It provides an alternative set of notes for those who may find school materials insufficient or difficult to follow.

At the same time, I believe that education should be accessible to everyone. Not all students can afford tuition, so this platform is open to anyone who wishes to learn, revise, and improve at their own pace.

In the future, I hope to expand this website to include more features, such as a discussion forum, progress tracking, quizzes, and interactive visualisations. However, with current academic commitments, development may take some time. Thank you for your patience and support.

docs.title: How to Use This Site
docs.text:
1. Open the sidebar
  2. Choose a subject
  3. Choose a chapter and read
  4. share with friends if you find this helpful :)

docs.title: Chapter Overview
docs.text: Learning Outcome 1(a) identify and describe protons, neutrons and electrons in terms of their relative charges and relative masses 1(b) deduce the behaviour of beams of protons, neutrons and electrons in an electric field 1(c) describe the distribution of mass and charges within an atom 1(d) deduce the numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons present in both atoms and ions given proton and nucleon numbers (and charge) 1(e) (i) describe the contribution of protons and neutrons to atomic nuclei in terms of proton number and nucleon number  (ii)distinguish between isotopes on the basis of different numbers of neutrons present 1(f) describe the number and relative energies of the s, p and d orbitals for the principal quantum numbers 1, 2 and 3 and also the 4s and 4p orbitals 1(g) describe the shapes of s, p and d orbitals (see also Section 13) [knowledge of wave functions is not required] 1(h) state the electronic configuration of atoms and ions given the proton number (and charge) 1(i) explain the factors influencing the ionisation energies of elements (see the Data Booklet) (see also Section 5) 1(j) deduce the electronic configurations of elements from successive ionisation energy data 1(k) interpret successive ionisation energy data of an element in terms of the position of that element within the Periodic Table 5(a) recognise variation in the electronic configurations across a Period and down a Group 5(b) describe and explain qualitatively the general trends and variations in atomic radius, ionic radius, first ionisation energy and electronegativity:  (i) across a Period in terms of shielding and nuclear charge  (ii) down a Group in terms of increasing number of electron shells, shielding and nuclear charge 6(a) define the terms relative atomic, isotopic, molecular and formula mass 6(c) calculate the relative atomic mass of an element given the relative abundances of its isotopes 13(a) explain that a transition element is a d block element whose atom has an incomplete d subshell, or which can give rise to cations with an incomplete d subshell 13(b) state the electronic configuration of a first row transition element and its ions (see also 1(h)) 13(c) explain why atomic radii and first ionisation energies of the transition elements are relatively invariant

docs.title: Atoms and Subatomic Particles
docs.text:
Learning Outcomes 1(a) identify and describe protons, neutrons and electrons in terms of their relative charges and relative masses 1(c) describe the distribution of mass and charges within an atom 1(d) deduce the numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons present in both atoms and ions given proton and nucleon numbers (and charge)

An atom is the smallest component of an element having the chemical properties of the element.

All atoms are made of up the same three subatomic particles

  • eletrons
  • protons
  • neutrons

The protons and neutrons make up the nucleus of the atom. Thus they are also referred to as nucleons. The electrons are surrounding the nucleus.

docs.title: Relative Mass and Charge of Subatomic Particles
docs.text: property proton electron neutron relative charge +1 -1 0 relative mass 1 \(\frac{1}{1836}\) 1

docs.title: Distribution of Mass and Charge in an Atom
docs.text:
So far, we know that

  • The protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus
  • The electrons have negligible mass compared to the protons and neutrons
  • The protons are positively charged
  • The electrons are negatively charged
  • The neutrons are neutral

Therefore, we can say that 99% of the mass is concentrated in the nucleus. All the positive charges are in the nucleus and the negative charges are on the electrons.

docs.title: Nuclide Notation
docs.text:
\[^{A}_{Z}\mathrm{X}\]

The proton number (Z) is the number of protons in the nucleus of each atom of an element.

In the atom, the number of electrons is also equal to the number of protons since atoms are neutral

The nucleon number (A), also called the mass number, is the total number of neutrons and protons in the nucleus of an atom of an element.

docs.title: Deflection of ions and charged particles in Electric Field
docs.text: Learning Outcome 1(b) deduce the behaviour of beams of protons, neutrons and electrons in an electric field

docs.title: Ions
docs.text:
A negative ion (anion) has more electrons than protons.

A positive ion (cation) has more protons than electrons.

docs.title: What happens when particles pass through electric field
docs.text:
Since like charges repel and unlike charges attract, negatively charged particles such as electrons will be attracted to the positive plate while positively charged particles such as protons will be attracted to the negative plate.

Neutron particles such as neutrons experience no attraction/repulsion and thus are not deflected.

However, if you have not realised, the proton and electron are deflected by different angles! More precise, angle 1 (electron) is greater than angle 2 (proton)

The reason is simple and requires a tiny bit of Physics.

Both the electron and proton carry charge of the same magnitude of 1. Thus they experience forces of the same magnitude!

Therefore, the one with a smaller mass will deflect by a larger angle (imagine if you push an elephant and a ball with the same force, the elephant won't move a inch and the ball will fly!).

Since we know that electrons have a relative mass is \(\frac{1}{1836}\) while that of protons is 1, the electrons will deflect by a much greater angle.

To summaries all this

\[ angle \propto \frac{charge}{mass} \]

docs.title: Isotopes
docs.text:
Learning Outcome 1(e) (ii)distinguish between isotopes on the basis of different numbers of neutrons present

Isotopes are atoms of an element that have the same proton number but different nucleon numbers (or same number of protons and different number of neutrons).

Isotopes of an element have the same electronic configuration and thus have similar chemical properties (undergo similar reactions).

They have different relative isotopic masses and thus different physical properties (density etc).

docs.title: Relative Masses
docs.text: Learning Outcomes 6(a) define the terms relative atomic, isotopic, molecular and formula mass 6(c) calculate the relative atomic mass of an element given the relative abundances of its isotopes

docs.title: Relative Atomic Mass
docs.text:
\[ Relative \; atomic \; mass, A_{r} = \; \frac{average \; mass \; of \; one \; atom \; of \; an \; element}{\frac{1}{12} \; \times \; the \; mass \; of \; one \; atom \; of \; ^{12}C} \]

The relative mass of atom of an element is the ratio of the average mass of one atom of the element to \(\frac{1}{12}\) times the mass of one atom of \(^{12}C\)

docs.title: Relative Isotopic Mass
docs.text:
\[ Relative \; isotopic \; mass, \; A_{r} \; = \; \frac{average \; mass \; of \; an \; isotope \; of \; an \; element}{\frac{1}{12} \; \times \; the \; mass \; of \; one \; atom \; of \; ^{12}C} \]

The relative mass of an isotope of an element is the ratio of the average mass of an isotope of the element to \(\frac{1}{12}\) times the mass of one atom of \(^{12}C\)

docs.title: Relative Abundance of Isotopes
docs.text:
The percentage of a particular isotope compared to the total number of atoms of that element in a mixture of isotopes is called the isotopic abundance or the relative abundance.

\[ Relative \, Atomic \, Mass, \, A_{r} \; = \; \sum (relative \; isotopic \; mass \; \times \; isotopic \; abundance) \]

docs.title: s, p and d blocks
docs.text:
The periodic table can be divided into s-block, p-block and d-block based on the electronic configuration of the atoms.

group number valence shell electronic configuration block 1 \(ns^1\) s 2 \(ns^2\) 3 \((n-1)d^1 \, ns^2\) d 4 \((n-1)d^2 \, ns^2\) 5 \((n-1)d^3 \, ns^2\) 6 \((n-1)d^4 \, ns^2\) 7 \((n-1)d^5 \, ns^2\) 8 \((n-1)d^6 \, ns^2\) 9 \((n-1)d^7 \, ns^2\) 10 \((n-1)d^8 \, ns^2\) 11 \((n-1)d^9 \, ns^2\) 12 \((n-1)d^{10} \, ns^2\) 13 \(ns^2 \, np^1\) p 14 \(ns^2 \, np^2\) 15 \(ns^2 \, np^3\) 16 \(ns^2 \, np^4\) 17 \(ns^2 \, np^5\) 18 \(ns^2 \, np^6\)

docs.title: Transition Metal
docs.text:
Defintion

A transition metal is a d-block element that form one or more stable ions with a partially filled d subshell.

Important

Zn and Sc are d block elements but they are not transition metal.

  • Zn forms \(Zn^{2+}\) with a fully filled d subshell \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^{10}\)
  • Sc forms \(Sc^{3+}\) with a empty d subshell \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6\)

docs.title: Electronic Configuration of Atoms
docs.text:
So far, we have learned principal quantum shells, subshells and orbitals and their relationship in term of energy level.

Now we are going to learn how electrons are allocated to the orbitals. The allocation is governed by 3 rules/laws/princiles.

docs.title: 1: The Aufbau Principle
docs.text:
Electrons in their ground states occupy orbitals in order of energy levels.

The orbital with the lowest energy level is always filled first.

Generally, in an atom, energy level increases with principal quantum number, 1s < 2s < 3s < ...

In the same principal quantum shell, energy level of subshells increases in the order of s, p, d, f; so 4s < 4p < 4d < 4d < ...

In the same principal subshell, orbitals are degenerate, so 3px = 3py = 3pz.

Important

Take note that 3d is more energetic than 4s when they are both empty. Therefore 4s is filled first 3d.

docs.title: Working Example
docs.text:
Suppose we want to find the electronic configuration of Carbon which has 6 electrons.

  1. We start from the subshell with the lowest energy level which is 1s. Since 1s can accomodate up to 2 electrons, we get \(1s^2\)
  2. Then we are left with 4 electrons to allocate, and the next subshell with the lowest energy level is 2s. Since 2s can accomodate up to 2 electrons, we get \(1s^2 \, 2s^2\)
  3. Finally, we are left with 2 electrons to allocate, and the next subshell with the lowest energy level is 2p. Since 2p can accomodate up to 6 elestrons, we obtain \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^2\)

docs.title: 2: Hund's Rule of Multiplicity
docs.text: When filling subshells that contain more than one orbital with the same energy, each orbital must be singly occupied before electrons are paired.

docs.title: Working Example
docs.text: Suppose we want to find the electronic configuration of Oxygen which has 8 electrons.

docs.title: Pauli Exclusion Principle
docs.text:
An orbital cannot hold more than two electrons and the two electrons in the same orbtital must be in opposite spins.

This is shown in electron-in-box diagram where the box represents orbital and the arrows represents electrons.

The direction of the arrow indicates the spin of the electron.

This is principle is demonstrated in the working example for Hund's Rule of Multiplicity.

docs.title: Cr
docs.text: The electronic configuration of Cr is \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^5 \, 4s^1\) (instead of \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^4 \, 4s^2\))

docs.title: Cu
docs.text: The electronic configuration of Cr is \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^10 \, 4s^1\) (instead of \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^9 \, 4s^2\))

docs.title: Cations
docs.text:
1. First write the electronic configuration of the atom
  2. Remove the number of electrons equal to the positive charge, starting from the highest energy subshell

Important

4s electrons are removed before 3d

docs.title: Working Example
docs.text:
Suppose we want to find the electronic configuration of \(Fe^{2+}\)

  1. First we find the electronic configuration of Fe, \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^6 \, 4s^2\)
  2. Remove 2 (since the charge is 2+) electrons, starting from the highest energy subshell, $1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2 \, 3p^6 \, 3d^6 $

docs.title: Anions
docs.text:
1. First write the electronic configuration of the atom
  2. Add the number of electrons equal to the negative charge, starting from the lowest energy available subshell

docs.title: Working Example
docs.text:
Suppose we want to find the eletronic configuration of \(N^{-3}\)

  1. First we find the electronic configuration of N, \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^3\)
  2. Add 3 (since the charge is -3) electrons, starting from the lowest energy available subshell which is found to be 2p, so \(1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6\)

docs.title: Principal Quantum Shells, Subshells and Orbitals
docs.text: Learning Outcomes 1(f) describe the number of the relative energies of the s, p and d orbitals for the principal quantum number 1, 2 and 3 and also the 4s and 4p orbitals

docs.title: Principal Quantum Shells
docs.text:
Previously in O level, we learn that electrons reside in electronic shells.

However, now we have levelled up to A level and we will call these shells as principal quantum shells.

Each principal quantum shell is assigned with a principal quantum number

As the principal quantum number increases, size, maximum number of electrons (\(2n^{2}\)), and energy level increases.

principal quantum number 1 2 3 4 maximum number of electrons (\(2n^{2}\)) 2 8 18 32

docs.title: Subshells
docs.text:
Tip

Think of principal quantum shells like different hotels of a particular chain. Subshells are the storeys in the hotels.

Each principal quantum shell can be divided into subshells. The number of subshells in a principal quantum shell is the same as its principal quantum number.

principal quantum number, n number of subshell types of subshells notation for subshells 1 1 s 1s 2 2 s, p 2s, 2p 3 3 s, p, d 3s, 3p, 3d 4 4 s, p, d, f 4s, 4p, 4d, 4f

In the same principal quantum shell, the energy level of subshells increases in the order of s < p < d < f.

For subshells in different principal quantum shells, subshells in quantum shells with higher principal quantum number have higher energy levels. That is 1s < 2s < 3s.

docs.title: Orbitals
docs.text:
An atomic orbital is the region of space with 90% probability of finding an electron.

  • Subshells have orbitals at the same energy level (degenerate) but different orientations in space.
  • Each orbital can contain up to 2 electrons.
subshell number of orbitals maximum number fo electrons in each subshell s 1 2 p 3 6 d 5 10 f 7 14

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: principal quantum number, n subshell number of orbitals maximum number of electrons in each principal quantum shell, \(2n^{2}\) 1 1s 1 2 2 2s  2p 1  3 8 3 3s  3p  3d 1  3  5 18 4 4s  4p  4d  4f 1  3  5  7 32

docs.title: Shapes of Orbitals
docs.text: Learning Outcomes 1(g) describe the shapes of s, p and d orbitals

docs.title: The s Orbital
docs.text:
Each s subshell only has one s orbital.

s orbitals are spherical in shape.

The size of s orbitals increase with the principal quantum number, 1s < 2s < 3s < ...

docs.title: The p Orbital
docs.text:
Each p subshell has three p orbitals.

The p orbital is dumb-bell in shape.

The three p orbitals in the same quantum shell are identical in size, shape and energy level (degenerate) but differ in their spatial orientation. The three p orbitals are named \(P_x, P_y, P_z\).

The size and energy level of p orbitals increase with the principal quantum number, so 2p < 3p < 4P <...

docs.title: The d Orbital
docs.text:
Each d subshell has five d orbitals.

The 5 d orbitals are degenerate.

The five d orbitals are named \(d_{xy}\), \(d_{xz}\), \(d_{yz}\), \(d_{x^2-y^2}\) and \(d_{z^2}\).

docs.title: Number of Quantum Shells and Effective Nuclear Charge
docs.text:
Electrons are held in the atom by electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons.

The strength of this electrostatic force of attraction results in difference in

  • atomic radii
  • ionic radii
  • ionisation energies
  • electronegativity

Therefore, it is important for us to understand how this electrostatic force of attraction varies across different elements.

The two factors that affect this attraction are

  • Number of quantum shells
  • Effective Nuclear Charge, \(Z_{eff}\)

docs.title: Number of quantum shells
docs.text: The number of quantum shells determines how far the outermost electron is from the nucleus. The further the outermost electron is from the nucleus, the weaker the attraction between the outermost electron and the nuclues. (If you happen to take H2 Physics, this is verified by the equation for electric force \(F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\))

docs.title: Effective Nuclear Charge
docs.text:
The effective nuclear charge, \(Z_{eff}\), is the net nuclear charge experienced by an outer electron. \(Z_{eff}\) depends on

  • Nuclear Charge (Z): The nuclear charge is dependent on the number of protons. The larger the number of protons, the greater the nuclear charge and the attraction for the outermost electrons by the nucleus.
  • Shielding Effect(S): Presence of inner shell electrons reduces the electrostatic attraction between the outermost electrons and the nucleus.
\[ Z_{eff} = Z - S \]

docs.title: Learning Outcome
docs.text: Learning Outcomes 16(a) recall and use appropriate circuit symbols 16(b) draw and interpret circuit diagrams containing sources, switches, resistors (fixed and variable), ammeters, voltmeters, lamps, thermistors, light-dependent resistors, diodes, capacitors and any other type of component referred to in the syllabus 16(c) define the resistance of a circuit component as the ratio of the potential difference across the component to the current in it, and solve problems using the equation \(V = IR\) 16(d) recall and solve problems using the equation relating resistance to resistivity, length and cross-sectional area, \(R = \frac{\rho l}{A}\) 16(e) sketch and interpret the I-V characteristics of various electrical components in a d.c. circuit, such as an ohmic resistor, a semiconductor diode, a filament lamp and a negative temperature coefficient (NTC) thermistor 16(f) explain the temperature dependence of the resistivity of typical metals (e.g. in a filament lamp) and semiconductors (e.g. in an NTC thermistor) in terms of the drift velocity and number density of charge carriers respectively 16(g) show an understanding of the effects of the internal resistance of a source of e.m.f. on the terminal potential difference and output power 16(h) solve problems using the formula for the combined resistance of two or more resistors in series 16(i) solve problems using the formula for the combined resistance of two or more resistors in parallel 16(j) solve problems involving series and parallel arrangements of resistors for one source of e.m.f., including potential divider circuits which may involve NTC thermistors and light-dependent resistors 16(k) solve problems using the formulae for the combined capacitance of two or more capacitors in series and in parallel 16(l) describe and represent the variation with time ... using equations of the form \(x = x_0 e^{-t/\tau}\) or \(x = x_0[1 - e^{-t/\tau}]\), where \(\tau = RC\) is the time constant
